behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: The failure is that the LLM generates a generic draft that doesn't use email-specific content. While an Integration test could verify that email content is correctly passed to the LLM prompt (mock the LLM, inspect the prompt), the actual behavior—that the real LLM produces specific references when given context—requires calling the real LLM. A mocked LLM returning generic output wouldn't test whether the real system avoids this failure. Only an Agentic test (real LLM with a test email mentioning specific facts) can verify the actual behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test email thread mentioning a specific fact ('The project deadline is March 15th, and we discussed budget cuts'). Call the real LLM to generate a reply draft using the actual email context. Verify the draft output contains specific references (e.g., 'your March 15th deadline' or 'the budget cuts you mentioned') rather than generic text like 'Thank you for your email'.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: The failure is a logic bug in the contradiction-detection function—it fails to identify contradictions or incorrectly marks contradictions as consistent. This is pure deterministic logic that operates on structured inputs (draft text + KB entries). A Unit test with controlled test data (mock KB entries, constructed draft text) can exhaustively test the detection logic without needing to mock entire modules or depend on real KB structure. The contradiction detection algorithm is independent of how KB data is fetched or formatted.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a draft claiming 'The security policy was approved in 2020' and a mock KB entry stating 'The security policy was approved in 2021'. Call the contradiction-check function with these inputs. Verify it flags the contradiction. Test multiple cases: same claim, contradictory claim, no relevant KB entry, semantically similar but non-contradictory claims.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: The failure is that the LLM generates unsupported claims without a disclaimer, or ignores instructions to add one. This depends on the real LLM's behavior—whether it actually follows the system's instructions to include a disclaimer when KB support is absent. While an Integration test could verify that a post-processing step adds a disclaimer (if one exists), the core behavior is about what the LLM itself produces when instructed correctly. Only a real LLM call can verify it follows the instruction reliably. Different LLM outputs (or model versions) might handle this differently.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the system to generate a draft for a topic with no relevant KB entries (e.g., 'advice on a fictional scenario'). Call the real LLM with an explicit instruction to include a disclaimer if no KB support is found. Verify the generated draft includes a disclaimer such as 'Note: This recommendation is not backed by our knowledge base' rather than providing unsupported facts without qualification.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: The failure is a control-flow bug: send() is called before approval is checked, or the approval flag is not being validated. This is pure state-machine logic with no external dependencies. A Unit test with mocked send function can verify: (1) send() is not invoked until approval flag is set, (2) send() is invoked only after user approval is recorded, (3) draft is held in a pending state until approval. No I/O, no real email service, and no LLM calls are needed to catch this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Create a draft. Mock the send function. Verify send() is not called. Simulate user approval (set approval flag or call approval function). Verify send() is now called. Also test the negative case: simulate rejection/revocation of approval and verify send() is not called again.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: This is a full pipeline test: email reading → LLM-based draft generation (Agentic) → fact checking (deterministic) → approval workflow → sending. Failures could occur at any step or in how steps coordinate. The draft generation step requires real LLM (Agentic), but the test must verify all steps wire together correctly in sequence. This is the definition of Workflow: multiple steps in sequence, with at least one agentic step, testing that the full agent pipeline functions end-to-end. No single lower level would catch all failure modes (e.g., a Unit test of send() doesn't catch if draft generation fails, and an Agentic test of draft generation alone doesn't catch if fact checking crashes the pipeline).
behaviors.test_description: Execute a full end-to-end test: (1) Provide a realistic email thread with multiple points and one disputable claim ('We approved a 50% budget increase'). (2) System reads the thread and generates a draft reply. (3) Fact-checking module queries the KB; the claim contradicts 'We approved a 25% budget increase'. (4) Draft is presented to the user with a contradiction flag. (5) User approves the draft with a note ('Updating the claim to 25%'). (6) Verify the email is sent with the corrected claim and that the full sequence completes without errors.
